content:
### Please read <#1418704743882297405> most recently letters for more context.

Red-hot tears stung Sol’s eyes, burning away the beautiful facade he always wore. The careful composure, the smile polished for courts and companions alike, all of it melted under the weight of his father’s venomous words.

Sol sat staring at the parchment far longer than he should have, his father’s words burning into him like acid. He had read letters like this before, full of expectation and venom, but this one cut deeper, because for the first time, he’d let himself be open and honest. He had spoken of Adrien with warmth, and for that, his father had carved him down to nothing. 

His eyes caught on the place where his father’s ink carved out the name Adrien, and he traced it once with his thumb, trembling. That single softness slipped into his letter had become the blade his father used to cut him down.

The tears broke then, falling heavily onto the parchment. His grief bled into the icy ink, warping the letters, smearing their cruelty into blurred shadows.

Abruptly, he shoved back his chair, boots striking too sharply against the stone as he stormed down the corridor. By the time he reached his room, the sobs broke loose; harsh, unsteady, audible, carrying the sound of someone who knew in his bones that his father would never truly understand or support him.

In his rush, the letter was left behind on the table, stained with blurred ink and salt. Its cruel words lay open in the glow of the lamplight; exposed and raw, waiting for someone else’s eyes to see what he could not bear to carry.

What Sol didn’t notice, as his sobs rattled the closed door to his room, was the soft slide of another envelope under the dorm’s front door. The paper was light and delicate, fragile in a way that made it feel almost out of place in the same room as the venomous ink left behind. Across the front, in graceful handwriting, it read: *From Cassiopeia Lyra Aelia*. His name was penned in that same gentle script, every curve of the letters carrying warmth his father’s hand never knew.

The seal gleamed in gold and silver wax, stamped with the crest of House Aelia: wings unfurled, circled with sun and moon blossoms. It rested quietly on the floor, waiting for someone to read it.

Caspian entered the dorm room a few minutes later. He looked down and saw the sealed letter just before he accidentally stepped on it. He leaned down and snagged it and examined it. 

He began to walk to Sol’s room when he walked past the table with the other letter left open. Without reading he saw the running ink and wet pages and he quickly scooped that up too. 

It seemed for him and his friends that when it rained it poured. Caspian slowly walked up to Sol’s door and started to hear the signs of heartbreak.

He knocked lightly and slipped the two letters beneath the door. 

“Hey Sol, found these outside. I didn’t read anything. I’m done with classes for the day, if you need someone to talk to just ask.”

The door opened slowly, Sol standing there with the letters pressed tight to his chest. His face looked flawless, too flawless. Not a trace of redness or tears, not even the faintest puffiness around his eyes. But there was a subtle shimmer at the edge of his features, like heat-haze on stone, the telltale ripple of an illusion spell stretched thin.

“Thanks, Cas,” he said lightly, voice warm and lilting, as though nothing in the world could be wrong. He lifted the letters just a little, making a show of them. “Would’ve hated to lose these.” He laughed, but the sound was thin and hollow.

Behind him, his wings twitched restlessly, betraying the composure on his face. For a brief moment his gaze dropped, shoulders sinking as though under some invisible weight, and the mask nearly slipped. When he looked up again, the smile was back; bright, determined, too practiced. “I’m fine. Really. Just…long day, you know?”

The words were hurried, the illusion flickering faintly as he said them, like the spell itself didn’t believe him. His knuckles whitened around the letters as he stepped back. “I’ll be fine, really. You don’t have to worry about me.”

Caspian crossed his arms unconvinced at the attempt from Sol. He looked at the letters that were being slightly crumpled under the aasimar’s grip.

“People who are fine don’t usually have to repeat the sentiment. You haven’t been here long enough to know that we’re not afraid of a few tears and hard emotions.” He gently shared with his roommate. 

He held up a finger to ask Sol to wait and he ducked into his own room. He summoned his echo to run into his room and drop his bags off and came back with a bottle of wine and a pair of glasses. He was starting to understand some of the positives to having a 

“Let’s have a glass of wine, and we can chat. The letters that have arrived from my family haven’t been much easier to deal with. Though yours seem to at least have your name in them”

Sol stood there with the two letters in hand, knuckles whitening slightly where he gripped the pages. Caspian’s words about wine lingered in the air, and for a moment he thought about trying to insist again that he was fine. But something in Caspian’s steady patience, in the way he offered rather than pressed, broke the fragile hold Sol had on the façade.

Then, with a slow exhale, he let it go. A small, weary wave of his hand unraveled the shimmer across his face, the glamour melting away. What remained was unvarnished: red-rimmed eyes, cheeks stained with tears, exhaustion carved deep where his usual smile had tried to live. He swallowed hard, lips twitching like he might still try to play it off, but no mask came, only the quiet that followed.

His gaze flicked toward the bottle in Caspian’s hand, and for the first time that night, a thin, almost shaky smile tugged at his mouth. “Wine sounds… good,” he admitted softly. “Better than pretending, at least.”

He turned without another word, padding into his room. When he returned, he carried an obsidian-black box cradled carefully in both hands. Its lid gleamed faintly in the firelight, silver moon phases etched across its surface, a container too elegant for the weight it held inside. Sol lowered himself onto the couch, the box set across his lap, fingers resting on it like it might burn him if he dared open it.

Caspian poured a nearly full glass to Sol and slid it across their small coffee table to him as he took a seat in one of the chairs across from the couch. He didn't flinch when the spell dropped revealing the telltale signs of an emotional toll he was far too familiar with. He poured himself a glass, less but still plenty. The glass tilted slightly around in a circle allowing the scent of the wine to be released. The fighter inhaled allowing the scent to fill his lungs and he exhaled with a sense of calm. It was deliberate, a scent that calmed him in difficult moments. There sure had been a lot of those lately.

He spoke quietly not with the typical confidence he usually brought to conversations, "Family isn't easy. When you shared about your family on your first day it was all too familiar. As I mentioned my relationship with my parents both figuratively and literally burned to the ground so I can understand a bit of where you are coming from."

Eying the box he was curious but didn't pry just yet but his gaze did linger on it.

Sol took the glass in his hand, staring into the dark red surface before he tipped it back and drank the whole thing in one go. The edge of his mouth quirked into a wry smile as he set the empty glass down, fingertips drumming lightly on the rim.

“Family,” he echoed, voice soft but tinged with something brittle. “You’re right. It’s not easy. And yours…” His gaze flicked to Caspian, lingering a beat. “Burning it to the ground sounds awful…and freeing. Though mine prefers chains over flames.” He reached down to the table, fingers tightening around the letter Sirius had sent. He turned it once, twice, the seal smudged where his tears had fallen, then extended it toward Caspian.

“If you really want to understand, read this,” he said quietly. “It’s easier to show you than to explain.”

His hand fell back to the obsidian box in his lap, thumb brushing over the silvered moon phases on the lid. He caught Caspian’s lingering gaze, and a faint chuckle escaped him, tired but genuine. “You want to know what’s inside, don’t you?”

Caspian leaned over and refilled the glass. He sipped his own glass and smiled as the liquid hit his taste buds.

“Freeing? Unfortunately not as much as you would hope. My doubts speak in their voice. That doesn’t ever go away I think.”

Reaching forward he took the letter when offered. Glass in one hand and letter in the other he flipped it open and began to read. 

As he read a variety of expressions crossed his face. He rolled his eyes multiple times, creased his brow in anger, and glanced up at Sol with sympathy.

“Your father sure is a vile piece of shit. Seems like someone who hasn’t been punched in the face before and it shows.”

He tossed the letter onto the table and set his glass atop it as a coaster. 

“That’s all that piece of paper is good for.”

A sympathetic pair of eyes met Sol’s. He tried a reassuring smile but his heart wasn’t in it. 

“Using your brother against you. That’s heinous. I’m sorry you have to go through that. I hope we can find a way to help him here at Spark-Oath.”

Then when the box was brought up Caspian let out a guilty sigh. 

“I’m sorry I know it’s inappropriate to pry but I really wanna know. Just look at it it’s so mysterious.”

Sol drank from the glass and let out a small, humorless laugh at Caspian’s words. “My doubts don’t sound like me either. They sound exactly like him. Every word in that letter is something I’ve already told myself a hundred times over. Seeing it in his writing makes it permanent.” His gaze flicked down to where Caspian’s glass now rested atop the parchment, and he smiled. “You’re right, though. That’s about all it’s good for.”

When Caspian called out the cruelty of using Lucien as a weapon, Sol’s throat tightened. His fingers hovered over the obsidian box, tracing one of the silver moon phases like a nervous habit. “That’s the sharpest blade he’s got. He knows I’d give up everything for my brother, and he makes sure I never forget it.”

His attention snagged suddenly on the second envelope he’d nearly forgotten about, the one written in his mother’s delicate script. He gave Caspian a sidelong glance before carefully breaking the silver-and-gold seal. “She probably wrote me just to smooth things over.”

He unfolded the light paper and read aloud, his voice slipping into a dry mimicry of hers: “Your father is under such strain, my dearest Sunni. He speaks with severity because he does not know how else to protect what is ours. Never doubt his love…”

Sol cut himself off with a bitter laugh, shaking his head. “There it is. She always finds a way to excuse him. Every venomous word, every demand, all wrapped in courtly pressure and *‘strain.’*” He dropped his gaze to the page, reading one last line under his breath before carefully folding it closed. “And that’s supposed to make it better.”

He set the letter down on the table, fingers tapping the edge once before withdrawing.

Sol pulled the obsidian box onto his lap, running his thumb over the silver moon phases carved into the lid. He looksd at Caspian, the faintest flicker of amusement tugging at his mouth.

When the box clicked open, it seemed almost laughably plain inside: just a bed of plush velvet padding, empty, unremarkable. Sol tilted it slightly toward Caspian, letting the emptiness sit for a beat before lifting his hand.

A shimmer of rainbow light rippled across the interior, dissolving the illusion. What lay beneath was anything but ordinary.

Neatly stacked, delicate parchment with an iridescent sheen rested on one side, bound together with a sheer ribbon the color of twilight. Beside them were vials and sachets, each holding rare and costly spell ingredients, carefully arranged as though they were treasures rather than tools.

Sol’s fingers brushed lightly over the ribbon, “Not much to look at, unless you know what you’re seeing,” he said softly, a soft smile lingering.

Sol glanced back at Caspian, a spark of something brighter slipping through the exhaustion. His fingers lingered over the ribbon-tied stack of iridescent parchment, and he carefully slid one page free, the sheen catching the lamplight like oil on water. His other hand hovered briefly over the nestled ingredients: moonstone dust sealed in glass, a dried sprig of evening primrose bound with twine, an opal that gleamed faintly, and a vial that looked as though it held liquid silver, thick as tears. He set them gently on the table before looking back at Caspian. “What’s your favorite memory; the last time you were truly happy? Tell me everything. Don’t leave out a detail.”

Caspian took another sip as Sol read the excerpt from his mother’s letter. He leaned back and rested his chin on his fist.

He kept a stoic expression about the contents and closed his eyes briefly to collect his thoughts.

“It’s not my place, but if you want this persons two copper it at least seems like she’s trying. I’m not making excuses she shouldn’t defend his behavior but taking the time to write you her own letter would count for something in my book.”

As the box was revealed to him he leaned in with unconscious excitement. When the box was initially revealed to be empty his smile dropped off his face as he fell for the trick. Hook, line, and sinker. The magic revealing esoteric components and brought the excitement back. 

“Nice trick.”

When asked what his favorite memory is he grew quiet and he sank into his seat a bit. He seemed nostalgic. It was clear that he had one in mind but it now it felt bittersweet.

“It was with someone important to me. She and I had snuck out of the palace to a bar where she performed and sang. When we returned to the palace that evening we climbed the old palace tower and sat on the roof dangling our legs off the side. We spent the entire night just talking and stargazing. We didn’t leave until the sun crested the horizon.” 

By the time he finished Caspian was bright red from sharing this very personal memory. He had decided to trust that this wasn’t another elaborate trick but was fully aware that this could just lead to teasing. He shifted uncomfortably hoping his trust wasn’t misplaced.

Sol sat quietly as Caspian began to speak, but his mother’s words lingered at the edges of his mind. *She’s trying.* Caspian was right about that, and some part of him wanted to believe it mattered. Yet every line that defended his father’s sharpness made the parchment feel heavier in his chest. A gentler voice didn’t erase venom. Still, maybe the act of writing at all counted for something.

As Caspian’s story unfolded, Sol lifted his hand. The bracer stirred instantly, its runes kindling with pale, gold light. Illusions rippled across the iridescent parchment in response to Caspian’s voice. When he said *palace roof*, a faint sketch of stone spires shimmered and faded. At *song*, glowing notes drifted across the page. *Stars* scattered like silver dust, *sunrise* bleeding faint amber over the edges before the images sank back into the parchment. Sol’s expression shifted with each; his mouth quirking at the rooftop, a soft chuckle escaping at the thought of dangling legs, his features warming at the mention of dawn creeping in.

The ingredients already laid out on the table quivered under the bracer’s pull. Moonstone dust rose and scattered across the parchment like tiny constellations. A dried sprig of primrose flared faintly as he set it down. The opal pulsed as though it remembered the story itself. Finally, Sol touched the vial, and a single drop of liquid silver spilled onto the page.

The parchment folded in on itself, collapsing into a sphere of small, iridescent light. The orb hovered for a breath before sinking into Sol’s bracer. Its runes flared brilliantly, and threads of gold, silver, and bronze spilled outward, wrapping around his wrists before unfurling into the air. They swirled gently, weightless, and then began to dance in a slow orbit, weaving a ring of shifting light around him and Caspian. The strands shimmered like starlight on water, casting the room in a soft glow as if memory itself had taken form.

Sol extended his hands, palms faced up, the radiant threads still twining between his fingers, and met Caspian’s gaze. His voice was steady but low, carrying something unguarded beneath it.

“Do you trust me?”

Caspian’s eyes grew wide as the dance of magic swirled around him. His head snapped in different directions trying to take in each element not wanting to miss a single step. 

“Yeah of course. This is amazing Sol. It was obvious you had some cool talents but this is on a whole other level.”

He reached out a tried to touch a few of the strands to get a sense of their essence. For a moment he didn’t even notice the extended hands he was so enraptured by everything else. 

When he had taken in the magic enough he placed his hands on top of Sol’s.

“So what happens now?”

Sol’s breath steadied as Caspian’s hands settled over his own, the glow of the threads warming between their palms. He closed his eyes, focusing on the pieces of memory he had pulled from Caspian’s story.

The golden, silver, and bronze threads stirred, then arched upward, weaving together like latticework until they formed a shimmering dome around the two of them. The glow shifted and pulsed, each strand humming softly as if alive, the dome enclosing them in a cocoon of light. Some of the threads slipped lower, curling gently around Caspian’s fingers as though binding him into the magic along with Sol.

Then the dome expanded. The threads flaring outward, stretching and unraveling as they wove the world into something else. The floor beneath them shuddered, shingles and stone settling into place as though rebuilt by unseen hands; the palace rooftop Caspian had described slowly appeared. The walls melted into distance, the ceiling dissolving into velvet night. Stars flooded the sky in glittering swaths, constellations so vivid they looked almost close enough to touch.

The air cooled with it, the illusion spilling into every sense. A breeze skimmed across their skin, carrying the faint scent of stone and the faraway sweetness of city smoke. From somewhere below, the ghost of a song rose; clear, bright notes echoing like they drifted up from a bar tucked far beneath their perch.

Though most of the threads dissolved, some lingered faintly at the edges; gold, silver, and bronze filaments woven into the horizon and the constellations above, as though the memory itself was stitched together by light.

By the time the last shimmer of magic sank back into Sol’s bracer, the illusion had fully taken hold. The dormitory was gone. In its place was Caspian’s memory, alive and whole around them; palace roof, open night, and sunrise waiting on the horizon.

Sol opened his eyes, the glow of magic still soft across his skin. His gaze found Caspian’s, a small, quiet smile tugging at his lips.

“This.”

Caspian’s eyes went wide once the effect completed. He walked around jaw open. Once his shock wore off he listened carefully to the music and smiled wistfully. 

“The music… it even sounds a bit like hers…”

He turned to Sol. He put his hand on the aasimar’s shoulder.

“Thank you for doing this, it’s beyond amazing. I don’t think you even realize how much this means to me. How is this even possible?”

Sol’s gaze followed Caspian as he moved through the illusion, a quiet satisfaction softening his features at the way wonder filled his friend’s eyes. When Caspian’s hand touched his shoulder and the question came, Sol’s fingers brushed lightly across the bracer at his wrist.

“My brother enchanted it,” he explained, voice gentler now, touched with nostalgia. “When court life became too much, he found a way for us to slip into something better. He’d say, if the world won’t give us peace, then we’ll make it ourselves. So he used our magic to weave a spell into the bracer, giving it the power to draw on our memories or those freely shared with us, and create illusions we could step inside and live as if they were real.” Sol looked at the bracer with bittersweet remembrance, “This is actually the last gift he gave me.”

Sol lowered himself onto the rooftop tiles, leaning back on his hands as the illusion’s cool breeze brushed over him. His eyes lifted to the painted horizon, colors shifting as the first light of sunrise spread across the sky. For a moment, he just breathed it in, letting the quiet settle.

A faint smile tugged at his lips. “You remind me of him, you know. My brother.” His gaze softened, caught somewhere between the memory and the moment beside Caspian.

“Lucien always carried the weight of others like it was second nature.” Sol’s fingers drummed lightly against the roof, as if echoing the heaviness of that thought. “Loyal to a fault, protective even when no one asked him to be…” He let out a quiet huff of air, something between a laugh and a sigh. “And so stubborn about doing what he thought was right.”

His eyes slid back to Caspian, studying him in the soft light of the illusion. “You’ve got that same steadiness in you. The same way of making people feel like they’re not alone, even when everything’s falling apart.”

The words lingered, stretching between them until Sol’s lips curved into something gentle; fondness edged with ache. “That’s why I trust you. Why this… feels easier with you here.”

For the first time in years, Sol felt something close to peace. Sitting on the rooftop of Caspian’s memory, he wasn’t weighed down by his father’s expectations or the endless pressure to perform. For once, he let himself just be; for once, he wasn’t an heir or son to be sharpened into a blade, he was just a friend. He was happy to be sitting beside someone who saw him, and not the name he carried.

He turned slightly, offering Caspian a faint but genuine smile. “You can call me Sunni, you know. All my close friends and family do. And after helping me through my mess with my parents… you’ve more than earned the right.”
+5 Development Points for all parties involved
